capDescription: 8-20 cm wide, bright crimson red fading to orange-red, covered in white to cream pyramidal warts (volva remnants). Sticky when wet.
gillDescription: White, free from the stem, crowded, broad. Short gills present.
stemDescription: 10-20 cm long, white, solid, base bulbous with concentric rings of cottony scales. Features a soft white hanging skirt-like ring.
lookAlikes: Amanita xanthocephala (Yellow-orange flygills, smaller, yellow cap parts)
notes: Introduced species, highly toxic/hallucinogenic. Forms spectacular mycorrhizal rings around Pinus radiata in Victorian plantations.

capDescription: 2-5 cm wide, conical to convex, hygrophanous. Caramel brown when moist, drying to pale straw yellow. Margin often translucent and slightly striated when wet.
gillDescription: Adnate to adnexed, close, pale brown turning dark purplish-brown as spores mature. Edges remaining paler.
stemDescription: 5-12 cm long, white to dingy silver-grey, fibrous, tough. Base often covered in dense white mycelial cords.
bruisingReaction: Intense blue-green bruising on all parts when handled or damaged.
lookAlikes: Galerina patagonica (Yellow-brown spore print, no blue bruising, central ring zone)
Hypholoma fasciculare (Sulfur tuft, bright yellow-green gills, bitter taste, no blue bruising)
notes: Native to SE Australia. Potently psychoactive (contains psilocybin). Can grow in abundance in municipal mulch gardens in Melbourne and Victoria hills.

capDescription: 3-8 cm wide, broadly conical to convex then flattening with a low umbo, reddish-cinnamon to golden-brown fading to pale yellow toward the margin. Bruises blue-green where damaged.
gillDescription: Adnate to adnexed, crowded, pale grey turning dark purplish-brown to almost black as spores mature; edges whitish.
stemDescription: 4-15 cm long, white (often yellowing or blue-staining with age), with a persistent membranous ring that becomes dusted dark with falling spores.
bruisingReaction: Bruises blue-green where handled or damaged.
lookAlikes: Psilocybe subaeruginosa (cooler southern climate, wood/mulch not dung, no persistent ring)
Protostropharia / Stropharia spp. (dung-loving but no blue bruising)
Panaeolus / Galerina spp. on dung (Galerina is DEADLY; no blue bruising)
notes: Pantropical dung-loving psilocybin species. In Australia it is mainly a SUBTROPICAL species of Queensland and northern NSW cattle pasture in the summer wet season, not the cool southern states. 'Golden Teacher' is a widely cultivated strain of this species. Potently psychoactive (contains psilocybin).

capDescription: 7-20 cm wide, funnel-shaped (infundibuliform) or fan-shaped. Creamy white with shades of purple, dark grey, or bronze near the center.
gillDescription: White to cream, deeply decurrent (running down the stem), crowded. Bioluminescent (glows soft green in complete darkness).
stemDescription: 2-8 cm long, lateral or off-center, thick, firm, matching cap color.
bruisingReaction: No bruising reaction, but gills emit a dim green light.
lookAlikes: Pleurotus ostreatus (Oyster Mushroom - edible, not native, gills not bioluminescent, typically uniform color)
notes: Highly toxic, causing severe vomiting. Celebrated for its remarkable bioluminescence due to luciferin-luciferase reaction, visible to dark-adapted eyes.

capDescription: 5-20 cm wide, convex then flat-convex, bright orange to tawny-yellow, dry, finely covered in tiny fibrillose scales. Fleshy and robust.
gillDescription: Adnate to decurrent with a tooth, yellow-orange turning rusty orange as spores ripen.
stemDescription: 8-15 cm long, thick, swollen in center or base, solid, yellowish-orange. Features a high, robust, membranous persistent rings.
lookAlikes: Armillaria luteobubalina (Australian Honey Fungus - white spore print, lacks intense orange color)
notes: Bitter taste. Often found in massive, visually stunning clusters at the base of Eucalyptus trees across Victoria.

capDescription: 0.5-2 cm wide, fan-shaped to kidney-shaped, bright orange to reddish-orange. Distinctly honeycomb-like or pore-like beneath, visible from above structurally.
gillDescription: Replaced by large, conspicuous hexagonal pores (0.5-1 mm wide), glowing brilliant orange.
stemDescription: 0.2-1.5 cm long, lateral, slender, bright orange, attaching Cap offset to wood.
lookAlikes: Cruentomycena viscidocruenta (Ruby Bonnet, tiny red cap, normal gills, not pores)
notes: Invasive species native to Madagascar. Rapidly spreading through wet gullies of Victoria (e.g. Otway Ranges, Dandenongs), displacing native saprotrophs.

substrates: Soil (mycorrhizal, especially with introduced oaks and other exotic trees)
capDescription: 5-15 cm wide, smooth, greenish-yellow to olive or bronze, sometimes pale to nearly white. Faint radial fibrils under the surface. Cap margin not striate.
gillDescription: White, free from the stem, crowded, remaining white at maturity.
stemDescription: White, with a soft hanging membranous ring near the top and a prominent sac-like cup (volva) enclosing the swollen base.
lookAlikes: Agaricus (field mushrooms) which have pink-then-brown gills and no volva
Edible straw mushrooms which also have a volva but pink-brown spores
notes: DEADLY. Responsible for the majority of fatal mushroom poisonings worldwide. Contains amatoxins that cause delayed, severe liver and kidney failure; cooking does not destroy them. Established in Victoria under introduced oaks in parks and gardens. Never handle and eat.

substrates: Soil in open forest and woodland
capDescription: 5-12 cm wide, opening from egg-shaped to broadly flat with a distinct central bump (umbo). Pale cream ground colour broken into concentric brown scales.
gillDescription: White to cream, free from the stem, crowded, soft.
stemDescription: Tall and slender with a swollen base, bearing a movable ring and a fine brownish snakeskin-like banding below the ring.
bruisingReaction: Little to no bruising; flesh may slowly discolour.
lookAlikes: Chlorophyllum molybdites, a toxic look-alike that produces a distinctly green spore print
Young Amanita species before the volva and ring are obvious
notes: A common native parasol of southern Australian forests. The dangerous confusion is with green-spored Chlorophyllum; always take a spore print. Treat as reference-only.

capDescription: 4-10 cm wide, deep violet to purple when young, slimy (viscid) when wet, fading to brownish-purple with age.
gillDescription: Violet at first, becoming rusty brown as the spores mature, attached to the stem. A cobweb-like veil (cortina) covers the young gills.
stemDescription: Stout, purple, club-shaped to bulbous at the base, often with a slimy universal veil layer.
lookAlikes: Other purple Cortinarius species, several of which are dangerously toxic
notes: A striking native webcap. Many Cortinarius worldwide contain orellanine, which causes delayed, irreversible kidney failure. The whole genus should be considered unsafe to eat.

capDescription: Tiny, 0.5-2 cm wide, brilliant sky-blue to pale blue, hemispherical, sticky, translucent with fine radial striations.
gillDescription: Pale blue to whitish, attached, well spaced.
stemDescription: Very thin, bluish and gelatinous, attaching to wood by a small basal disc.
lookAlikes: Other small Mycena, none of which share the vivid blue colouring
notes: An iconic Gondwanan species found in wet gullies of the Otways, Dandenongs, and Tasmania. Grows in colonies on damp rotting wood. Too small and insubstantial to be of food interest.

capDescription: Emerges from a white gelatinous egg into a red star 5-10 cm across with six to ten arms radiating from a central disc coated in dark olive-brown spore slime.
gillDescription: None. As a stinkhorn it has no gills; spores are carried in the foul-smelling slime (gleba).
stemDescription: A short, hollow, white to pinkish spongy stalk rising from the ruptured egg.
lookAlikes: Other stinkhorns such as Clathrus and Lysurus species
notes: A native stinkhorn that smells of carrion to attract flies, which disperse the spores. Common in mulched gardens after rain. Not edible.

capDescription: 2-6 cm wide, sulphur-yellow with a tawny to orange-brown centre, smooth, convex then flattening.
gillDescription: Crowded, sulphur-yellow becoming greenish then purple-brown as spores ripen, attached to the stem.
stemDescription: Slender, yellow above and rusty toward the base, often curved, clustered, with only a faint ring zone.
bruisingReaction: No bruising; flesh is intensely bitter.
lookAlikes: Edible honey fungus, which has a white spore print
notes: Toxic and very bitter. Grows in dense clusters on dead wood. Among the most common toxic mushrooms encountered in Victorian forests and parks.

capDescription: Tall and cylindrical, 5-15 cm high, white with shaggy upturned scales. The lower edge blackens and dissolves into an inky black liquid as it matures (deliquescence).
gillDescription: Crowded, white then pink, finally turning black and dissolving from the rim upward.
stemDescription: White, hollow, tall, with a movable ring low on the stalk.
bruisingReaction: Auto-digests to black ink rather than bruising.
lookAlikes: Coprinopsis atramentaria (Common Ink Cap), which causes illness if consumed with alcohol
notes: Distinctive for its shaggy white cap that liquefies into ink. Appears quickly on lawns and disturbed ground after rain. Reference-only in this app.

capDescription: Thin, leathery, fan-shaped brackets 3-8 cm across in overlapping tiers, banded in concentric zones of brown, tan, grey, rust and bluish tones, with a velvety surface.
gillDescription: No gills. The underside is a whitish layer of very fine pores.
stemDescription: Absent; attached laterally to the wood.
lookAlikes: Stereum species, which have a smooth (poreless) underside
notes: A widespread saprotrophic bracket fungus that recycles dead hardwood. Persistent year-round. Tough and woody rather than a culinary mushroom.

capDescription: 5-20 cm wide, oyster or fan-shaped, smooth, ranging from grey-brown to creamy fawn, often in shelving clusters.
gillDescription: White to cream, decurrent (running down onto the short stem), fairly well spaced.
stemDescription: Short and stubby, lateral or nearly absent, often merging with neighbouring caps.
lookAlikes: Omphalotus nidiformis (Ghost Fungus), a toxic look-alike with crowded bioluminescent gills, usually in dense clusters at the tree base
notes: A widely cultivated edible, but in the field its toxic look-alike the Ghost Fungus is genuinely dangerous. Expert confirmation is essential before any consumption; this app is for reference only.

substrates: Base of living and dead eucalypts
capDescription: 3-10 cm wide, honey-yellow to tan-brown, often with fine darker scales toward the centre, convex then flattening, appearing in dense clusters.
gillDescription: Cream, attached to slightly decurrent, bruising brownish with age.
stemDescription: Fibrous and tapering, sharing a clustered base, bearing a yellowish ring high on the stalk.
lookAlikes: Galerina species, which are small and deadly
notes: A major native forest and garden pathogen causing Armillaria root rot, killing eucalypts and ornamentals. Identified by clustered honey-coloured caps with a ring and white spores.

capDescription: 5-15 cm, orange to carrot-orange with concentric darker zones, often greening with age; depressed centre, inrolled margin when young.
gillDescription: Decurrent, orange, exuding carrot-orange milk (latex) when cut.
stemDescription: 3-8 cm, orange, often with darker pitted spots (scrobicules), hollow with age.
bruisingReaction: Bruises and stains dull green where handled.
notes: Sought-after edible that fruits in Victorian radiata pine plantations; the carrot-orange latex and green staining are key. Never eat any fungus on AI ID alone.

capDescription: 5-12 cm, chocolate to chestnut brown, slimy/glutinous when wet, smooth.
gillDescription: Pore surface (no gills) — yellow, fine pores; a purple-brown membranous ring sheaths the upper stem.
stemDescription: 4-8 cm, pale yellow above the ring, dotted below.
bruisingReaction: Pores may dull slightly; flesh barely changes.
notes: Common introduced bolete under pines; edible after peeling the slimy cap skin, though it can be laxative. Remove pore layer when older.

capDescription: 4-10 cm, cinnamon to ochre-brown, slimy when wet, smooth.
gillDescription: Pore surface yellow, exuding milky droplets when young; NO ring on stem.
stemDescription: 3-6 cm, pale yellow, granular-dotted near the apex.
notes: Like Slippery Jack but ringless, with weeping young pores. Introduced under pines; edible after removing the slimy skin.

capDescription: Up to 40+ cm and several kg — one of the world's largest boletes; olive-brown to tan, dry, often lumpy.
gillDescription: Pore surface (no gills) olive-yellow, bruising blue then brown.
stemDescription: Stout, bulbous, brown, very thick.
bruisingReaction: Flesh and pores bruise blue-green rapidly.
notes: A spectacular giant bolete found around eucalypts and gardens in southern Australia. Edibility uncertain/poor — treat as inedible.

capDescription: 3-8 cm, bright orange to vermilion with a yellow flush, bearing yellow patchy veil remnants; margin striate.
gillDescription: White, free, crowded.
stemDescription: 5-10 cm, white to pale yellow, with a yellow basal volva; ring usually absent or fleeting.
notes: A common native Amanita of eucalypt forests. As with all Amanita, assume toxic — the genus contains the deadliest fungi.

capDescription: 3-8 cm, distinctive olive to apple-green, dry, finely fibrillose, often cracking.
gillDescription: Yellow-brown becoming rusty as spores mature; cortina (cobweb veil) when young.
stemDescription: 4-8 cm, pale yellow-green, dry.
notes: Striking green native webcap. Webcaps (Cortinarius) include species with dangerous kidney toxins — not for eating.

capDescription: 2-6 cm, glossy blue to turquoise when young, a yellow-ochre disc developing at the centre with age; viscid.
gillDescription: Bluish then rusty-brown with spores; cortina present.
stemDescription: 3-7 cm, blue fading to whitish, with a slightly bulbous base.
notes: A jewel-like blue webcap of southern forests. Cortinarius are not considered edible — several are seriously toxic.

capDescription: Erect, repeatedly branched coral-like clusters 4-15 cm tall; ochre, apricot, pink or violet tones, tips often brighter.
gillDescription: No gills — spores borne on the smooth branch surfaces.
stemDescription: A short, thick common base from which the branches arise.
notes: Branched coral fungi on forest floors. Several Ramaria are laxative or mildly toxic; identification to species is difficult — not recommended to eat.

capDescription: Slender unbranched clubs/spindles 3-8 cm tall, bright orange to apricot, often grooved, in scattered troops.
gillDescription: No gills — smooth fertile surface.
stemDescription: Tapered base, same colour.
notes: Vivid orange clubs dotting mossy ground in autumn. Harmless but of no culinary value.

capDescription: 1-4 cm, brilliant scarlet to orange-red, dry to slightly scurfy, fading with age.
gillDescription: Waxy, thick, reddish to orange, adnate to slightly decurrent.
stemDescription: 2-5 cm, slender, red to orange, dry.
notes: A tiny brilliant waxcap of mossy forest floors. Waxcaps are generally regarded as inedible; admire, don't eat.

capDescription: 1-4 cm, an intense sky-blue to turquoise, conical to bell-shaped, finely silky, fading to greenish.
gillDescription: Adnexed, whitish then salmon-pink as the pink spores mature.
stemDescription: 3-7 cm, blue, slender, fibrous.
notes: An electric-blue pinkgill prized by fungi photographers. Entoloma contains toxic species — not edible.

capDescription: 1.5-5 cm, tawny-orange to lilac/amethyst, hygrophanous (fading as it dries), often with a wavy striate margin.
gillDescription: Thick, widely spaced, decurrent, flesh-pink to lilac, dusted white with spores.
stemDescription: 3-8 cm, fibrous, tough, same colour as cap, often twisted.
notes: The 'deceivers' vary wildly in colour, making them hard to pin down. Edible kinds exist overseas but Australian material is best left for study.

capDescription: 4-10 cm, often blood-red, purple or yellow, the cuticle peeling at the margin; flesh notably brittle/crumbly.
gillDescription: White to cream, brittle, fairly crowded.
stemDescription: 3-7 cm, white, chalky and snapping cleanly like chalk.
bruisingReaction: Little change; some redden or grey.
lookAlikes: Many Russula species — notoriously hard to separate
notes: Brittlegills snap like chalk and are a huge, difficult genus. Some are edible, some sharply acrid; species ID requires microscopy — don't eat.

capDescription: 2-6 cm, pear-shaped, white to grey-brown, covered in conical pyramidal spines that rub off leaving a net pattern; a central pore opens at maturity to puff brown spores.
gillDescription: No gills — interior a solid white 'gleba' that turns olive-brown and powdery.
stemDescription: Short sterile stem-like base.
lookAlikes: Young Amanita egg (cut to check for gills!)
notes: Edible only when the inside is pure firm white; once yellow/brown it is past it. ALWAYS slice top-to-bottom to rule out a developing Amanita.

substrates: Soil on banks and trail edges
capDescription: A 1-2 cm spore sac on a gelatinous stalk; dark brown head crowned by a striking raised RED star-shaped 'mouth'.
gillDescription: No gills — interior powdery spore mass.
stemDescription: Spongy, gelatinous, lattice-like reddish-brown column 2-6 cm.
notes: An unmistakable curiosity with a lipstick-red mouth, common on damp earthy banks. Not edible.

capDescription: Emerges from a white 'egg' as a hollow white lattice ball (cage) 6-12 cm, which can detach and roll.
gillDescription: No gills — olive, foul-smelling spore slime (gleba) coats the inner lattice to attract flies.
stemDescription: No true stem; the cage sits on egg remnants.
notes: A stinkhorn relative that forms a rolling white cage with a carrion smell. Not edible; the immature 'egg' is the only edible-overseas stage but best avoided.

substrates: Dead attached wood (parasitic on crust fungi)
capDescription: Gelatinous, folded, brain-like lobes 2-7 cm, bright golden-yellow to orange, drying to a hard dark scale and reviving when wet.
gillDescription: No gills — smooth gelatinous fertile surface.
stemDescription: No stem; grows directly on wood.
notes: A jelly fungus brightening dead branches after rain, parasitic on wood-crust fungi. Edible but tasteless; mainly of interest as a study/medicinal species.

capDescription: 1-4 cm fan/shell-shaped brackets, greyish-white and densely hairy above, attached without a stem; curls when dry.
gillDescription: Radiating 'gills' that are uniquely split lengthwise and roll back in dry weather.
stemDescription: No stem (attached at one edge).
lookAlikes: Stereum and Trametes brackets (no split gills)
notes: The world's most widespread fungus; the lengthwise-split gills are diagnostic. Tough and inedible (though eaten in some cultures).

capDescription: Erect, antler-like or simple black prongs 2-6 cm, dusted white-grey with powdery asexual spores at the tips (like a snuffed wick), blackening with age.
gillDescription: No gills — flask-shaped fruiting bodies embedded in the black tissue.
stemDescription: Tough, wiry black base.
notes: A common woodland 'pyrenomycete' on rotting wood year-round. Inedible (woody).

capDescription: Large hard perennial bracket 10-50+ cm, concentrically furrowed, grey-brown crust above; flattened, woody.
gillDescription: Pore surface white, instantly bruising brown when scratched — the classic 'drawing' surface.
stemDescription: No stem; broadly attached to wood.
bruisingReaction: White pore surface bruises brown to the touch.
notes: A woody perennial bracket whose white underside can be drawn on (it bruises brown). Inedible but used medicinally and as a natural canvas.

capDescription: Large soft white to cream bracket or knob up to 30 cm, becoming chalky and crumbly when old.
gillDescription: Pore surface white, fine.
stemDescription: No stem; bracketed on eucalypts.
notes: A soft white eucalypt bracket; dried punk was carried by Aboriginal people to transport fire. Not a culinary species (unlike the orange northern 'chicken of the woods').

capDescription: Small fan/spoon-shaped brackets 1-3 cm, vivid orange to brick, leathery, attached by a tiny lateral point.
gillDescription: Thick, widely spaced, radiating orange gills.
stemDescription: No real stem (lateral attachment).
notes: Bright orange leathery fans crowding fallen twigs in wet forest. Inedible.

capDescription: 1-4 cm, pleated/grooved parachute cap, tan to reddish-brown with a darker centre, dry and velvety.
gillDescription: Whitish, distant, attached to a tiny collar.
stemDescription: 3-8 cm, wiry, dark brown, velvety, rooting into litter.
notes: A delicate litter-dwelling parachute that revives after rain. Too small and tough to eat.

capDescription: Clusters of golf-ball-like orange globes 1-3 cm, pitted with golf-ball dimples, emerging from swollen galls on beech branches in spring.
gillDescription: No gills — each dimple is a spore cup.
stemDescription: No stem; clustered on host galls.
notes: A spring fungus found only on Myrtle Beech in cool Victorian rainforest (e.g. the Otways, Yarra Ranges). Edible historically but ecologically significant — leave it.

substrates: Dead wood, mossy logs, wood chips
capDescription: 1.5-4 cm, tawny to ochre-brown, hygrophanous (drying paler), smooth, bell-shaped to flat with a low umbo.
gillDescription: Adnate, rusty-brown, fairly crowded.
stemDescription: 2-6 cm, brown, with a thin ring zone and whitish fibrils below.
notes: DEADLY — contains the same amatoxins as the Death Cap and has killed people who mistook it for edible or psychoactive wood-loving mushrooms. Note: it does NOT bruise blue.

capDescription: 6-15 cm, white to pale grey, covered in thick floccose white veil patches; margin appendiculate.
gillDescription: White, free, crowded.
stemDescription: 8-16 cm, white, with a large saccate volva and floccose ring zone.
notes: A large native eucalypt-forest Amanita. As with the whole genus, assume toxic — never eaten.

capDescription: 4-9 cm, grey to grey-brown, smooth, margin strongly striate; ringless.
gillDescription: White, free.
stemDescription: 6-12 cm, slender, white-grey, with a sac-like volva; NO ring.
notes: A ringless native grisette. Even ringless Amanita are not recommended; ID is risky.

capDescription: 5-12 cm, tan to ochre-brown, funnel-shaped with an inrolled margin, dry.
gillDescription: Decurrent, yellow-brown, easily separable from the cap, sometimes forking.
stemDescription: 3-7 cm, central, paler than cap.
notes: Native relative of the dangerous Brown Roll-rim. Treat as toxic — Paxillus relatives cause a fatal immune reaction.

capDescription: 1.5-5 cm, brick to tawny-orange, hygrophanous, striate margin, scurfy centre.
gillDescription: Thick, distant, pinkish-flesh, decurrent, white-dusted.
stemDescription: 3-8 cm, fibrous, tough, cap-coloured.
notes: A common tough little 'deceiver' of southern soils. Study species; not a recommended edible here.

capDescription: 1-4 cm, bright chrome-yellow to lemon, viscid, conico-convex.
gillDescription: Waxy, yellow, adnate to decurrent.
stemDescription: 2-5 cm, yellow, slender, slimy.
notes: A glossy lemon waxcap of mossy wet forest. Waxcaps are regarded as inedible — leave them.

capDescription: 2-6 cm, apricot to salmon-orange, dry to slightly greasy, convex then flattening.
gillDescription: Thick, waxy, pale apricot, decurrent.
stemDescription: 3-6 cm, paler, dry.
notes: An indicator of old, unfertilised grassland. Inedible; valued ecologically.

capDescription: 3-7 cm, lilac to steel-blue with a metallic sheen, viscid, fading ochre.
gillDescription: Pale violet then rusty; cortina present.
stemDescription: 4-8 cm, bluish, club-based.
notes: One of many blue native webcaps. Cortinarius are not eaten — some carry slow kidney toxins.

capDescription: 2-5 cm, bright yellow to orange-yellow, dry, silky-fibrillose.
gillDescription: Bright yellow then rusty; cortina present.
stemDescription: 3-7 cm, yellow, dry.
notes: Vivid yellow webcaps once used as dyes. Like all Cortinarius, not for eating.

capDescription: 3-9 cm, deep pink-red to rhubarb, dry and finely scaly/cracked.
gillDescription: Pore surface (no gills) yellow, angular pores.
stemDescription: 5-11 cm, pink-red, often ribbed at the apex.
notes: A striking pink-red native bolete. Edibility poorly known — treat as inedible.

capDescription: 4-10 cm, brown to grey-brown, dry, often wrinkled.
gillDescription: Pore surface white then pink-brown; deeply pitted/lacunose stem.
stemDescription: 6-14 cm, conspicuously pitted and netted, pale.
notes: Recognised by the strongly pitted stem. Not regarded as a culinary species.

substrates: Living and dead hardwood trunks (often eucalypt, oak)
capDescription: 10-25 cm, tongue or liver-shaped bracket, blood-red to liver-brown, moist and gelatinous, oozing red juice.
gillDescription: Pore surface cream, the tubes individually separable.
stemDescription: Lateral, short or absent.
bruisingReaction: Cut flesh is marbled red like raw meat and bleeds a reddish juice.
notes: An edible bracket when young (acidic, best cooked), but it stains and can be tough. Confirm ID with an expert.

capDescription: Hard bracket 5-20 cm, yellow-brown to ochre, smelling strongly of curry when fresh.
gillDescription: Pore surface yellowish.
stemDescription: No stem; bracketed.
notes: A woody eucalypt bracket famous for its curry smell. Inedible.

capDescription: 2-7 cm thin fan brackets, concentrically zoned tan/orange/brown, silky above.
gillDescription: Underside SMOOTH (no pores) — the key difference from true Turkey Tail.
stemDescription: No stem; attached at one edge.
notes: Looks like Turkey Tail but the underside is smooth, not pored. Inedible (woody).

capDescription: Ear-shaped gelatinous brackets 2-8 cm, tan to red-brown, velvety outside, smooth and veined inside; dries hard, revives when wet.
gillDescription: No gills — smooth fertile inner surface.
stemDescription: No stem; attached laterally.
notes: The southern 'wood ear', related to the cultivated culinary species; edible cooked but bland. Confirm ID first.

capDescription: Erect gelatinous-tough yellow to orange antler-like prongs 1-4 cm, sticky.
gillDescription: No gills — smooth fertile surface.
stemDescription: Tapered base on wood.
notes: Rubbery orange jelly-clubs on rotting wood. Harmless but inedible.

capDescription: Tufts of brittle, branched amethyst-violet clubs 3-10 cm tall.
gillDescription: No gills — smooth fertile branch surface.
stemDescription: Fused violet base.
notes: A spectacular violet coral of mossy ground. Harmless; not worth eating.

capDescription: Onion-shaped, splitting into a star of 4-8 rays that fold back, lifting a central spore sac that sits in a 'collar'; 4-10 cm across.
gillDescription: No gills — central sac puffs brown spores from an apical pore.
stemDescription: No stem.
notes: An unmistakable earthstar; the cracked collar is diagnostic. Inedible.

capDescription: Club to pear-shaped 5-20 cm, yellow-brown, the interior packed with pea-like 'peridioles' in a black tarry matrix.
gillDescription: No gills — internal spore mass weathering from the top down.
stemDescription: Rooting yellow base.
bruisingReaction: Yellow surface stains the hands; used as a natural dye.
notes: A tough mycorrhizal ball, important in reforestation and a traditional dye. Not edible.

capDescription: Firm rounded ball 2-8 cm, ochre to brown, thick warty rind that cracks; cuts to a HARD purple-black marbled interior.
gillDescription: No gills — solid firm gleba, purple-black then powdery.
stemDescription: No real stem.
bruisingReaction: Rind may yellow then redden when cut.
notes: TOXIC if eaten — the firm dark interior separates earthballs from edible puffballs (which are soft and white inside). Always cut to check.

capDescription: 5-12 cm, lilac to violet-brown fading tan, smooth, margin inrolled then flat.
gillDescription: Crowded, violet, sinuate, fading buff.
stemDescription: 4-8 cm, lilac, fibrous, bulbous base.
notes: An edible exotic blewit (cook thoroughly) — but violet Cortinarius lookalikes are dangerous, so spore print and care are essential.

capDescription: 5-15 cm, white, smooth, squarish when young; bruises CHROME-YELLOW especially at the cap edge and stem base.
gillDescription: Crowded, free, pink then chocolate-brown.
stemDescription: 5-12 cm, white, with a ring; base bruises bright yellow and smells of phenol/ink.
bruisingReaction: Stains bright chrome-yellow, strongest at the stem base; unpleasant chemical smell.
notes: TOXIC — the commonest mushroom-poisoning culprit in gardens. The yellow staining base + phenol smell distinguish it from edible Agaricus.

substrates: Soil in unimproved pasture and lawns
capDescription: 4-10 cm, white to cream, dry, silky to slightly scaly.
gillDescription: Crowded, free, bright PINK when young then chocolate-brown.
stemDescription: 3-6 cm, white, with a thin fragile ring; base does NOT yellow.
bruisingReaction: Flesh may slowly pink; does not stain chrome-yellow.
notes: The classic edible field mushroom — but young pink gills are vital: white gills + a volva mean a potentially deadly Amanita. Always check the base.

capDescription: 2-5 cm, tan to buff, bell-shaped then flat with a broad umbo, hygrophanous.
gillDescription: Whitish, broad, well-spaced, almost free.
stemDescription: 3-7 cm, tough, pale, pliant (bends without snapping).
lookAlikes: Toxic lawn Clitocybe (white, crowded decurrent gills)
notes: A grassland edible that forms fairy rings — but deadly white Clitocybe also grow in lawns, so the tough pliant stem and spacing must be confirmed.

capDescription: 2-6 cm, tawny-orange to brown, smooth, hygrophanous, in dense clusters.
gillDescription: Crowded, olive then purple-brown.
stemDescription: 4-9 cm, brown, fibrous, ring zone.
notes: Cluster-forming woodtuft. Easily confused with the DEADLY Galerina — not for eating.

capDescription: 1-3 cm kidney/fan brackets, tan to buff, dry and scurfy, attached by a tiny lateral stub.
gillDescription: Crowded, pale cinnamon, gills faintly glowing green in the dark in some strains.
stemDescription: Rudimentary lateral stem.
notes: A tiny bracket; some populations are bioluminescent. Acrid and inedible.

capDescription: 4-14 cm, shell to fan-shaped, cream to grey-brown, smooth, margin inrolled.
gillDescription: White, decurrent, running down the short lateral stem.
stemDescription: Short, lateral or absent, white.
notes: Native oyster mushrooms grow in shelves on wood. CAUTION: the toxic Ghost Fungus is a lookalike — it glows green in the dark and has more crowded gills.

capDescription: 5-12 cm, ochre to tan, viscid, robust, margin inrolled.
gillDescription: Pale clay then rusty; cortina present.
stemDescription: 5-10 cm, stout, white, bulbous.
notes: A robust webcap of eucalypt soils. Cortinarius are not eaten; several are seriously toxic.

capDescription: 0.5-2 cm, conical to bell-shaped bonnet, grey-brown to pinkish, translucent-striate.
gillDescription: Whitish to grey, adnate, distant.
stemDescription: 2-6 cm, very slender, fragile, often rooting.
bruisingReaction: Some Mycena exude juice when broken.
notes: Delicate litter bonnets. Too small and often toxic/unknown — not for eating.

capDescription: 1-3 cm, rusty to tawny-red, hygrophanous, convex then flat.
gillDescription: Adnate to slightly decurrent, ochre-brown, fairly distant.
stemDescription: 2-5 cm, reddish, fibrillose.
notes: A common mulch-bed 'little brown mushroom'. ID to species is hard; not eaten.

capDescription: 1.5-6 cm, vivid orange-red to brick, smooth and slightly sticky, with whitish veil flecks at the margin.
gillDescription: Adnate, grey then purple-brown.
stemDescription: 3-7 cm, white-yellowish, with a faint ring zone, orange base.
notes: The ubiquitous bright orange-red mushroom of garden wood-chip beds. Regarded as inedible/suspect.

capDescription: 1.5-4 cm, hemispherical to broadly convex, light grey to tan, often cracking in dry weather; bruises blue where damaged.
gillDescription: Adnate, mottled grey turning black as spores ripen; edges whitish.
stemDescription: 7-12 cm, slender, greyish to flesh-pink, bruising blue-green toward the base; no ring.
bruisingReaction: Bruises blue where handled or damaged.
lookAlikes: Panaeolus / Panaeolina spp. on dung (no blue bruising)
notes: Pantropical dung species; in Australia mainly subtropical Queensland and northern NSW. In Australia 'Blue Meanies' usually refers to this strongly blue-bruising Panaeolus — though the name is also used loosely for a blue-bruising Psilocybe cubensis, so it is ambiguous. Contains psilocybin — potently psychoactive.

capDescription: 2-5 cm, chestnut to caramel-brown, strongly hygrophanous, with a distinctly wavy (undulating) margin when mature; drying pale buff.
gillDescription: Adnate to sinuate, cinnamon-brown maturing dark purplish-brown; edges paler.
stemDescription: 3-8 cm, white, fibrous, slightly enlarged at the base, bruising blue-green.
bruisingReaction: Bruises blue-green where handled or damaged.
lookAlikes: Psilocybe subaeruginosa (more slender, less wavy margin)
Galerina marginata (DEADLY — rust-brown spores, no blue bruising)
notes: An introduced temperate wood-chip Psilocybe established in cooler southern Australia (mulched garden beds, parks). 'Wavy caps' refers to the undulating mature cap margin. Contains psilocybin — potently psychoactive. Easily confused with the DEADLY Galerina marginata, which does NOT bruise blue.
